narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 and LSAT score of 30. The admissions model evaluated your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, predicting a 65.1% chance that you would fail, which tipped the decision against admitting you at this time. This prediction hinges on how your academic history stacks up against patterns seen in thousands of past students, where stronger early performance often correlates with bar success. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical 5.53 average, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lower tier compared to peers who passed the bar. Nearly as important was your Year 1 decile of 3, again under the average of 5.71, which reinforced concerns about consistently middling grades from the start. While your LSAT score worked somewhat in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing—unlike lower scores that drag others down—its positive effect wasn't strong enough to offset those early academic signals. Overall, your grades positioned you below the typical successful applicant, whose deciles and scores align more closely with bar passers. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background, where many have succeeded with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it above the 36.7 average, perhaps through dedicated prep courses, and consider graduate-level courses or work experience to demonstrate stronger academic momentum. Building a narrative around recent achievements could also help in reapplying or appealing to schools with holistic reviews. With persistence, students like you often turn predictions around by addressing these key gaps head-on.
